'''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡}} {{ill|Chatan Town|ja|北谷町}} Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="注釈">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|Okinawa War|ja|沖縄戦}} and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of {{ill|Kadena Air Base|ja|嘉手納飛行場}}, so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}} and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar December 24|lt_ja=Lunar December24日}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar February 2|lt_ja=Lunar February2日}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.

In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar February 2|lt_ja=Lunar February2日}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

[[Category:Religious Facilities in Okinawa]] [[Category:Ryukyuan Religion]] [[Category:Buildings in Chatan Town]] [[Category:Ryukyu Shinto]] [[ja:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[Category:Ryukyu Shinto]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]] [[ja:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category: Tier 1]] [[ja:下勢頭集落の拝所]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shimosedo Settlement Uganju}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shimosedo Settlement Uganju}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

'''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡}} {{ill|Chatan Town|ja|北谷町}} Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="注釈">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|Okinawa War|ja|沖縄戦}} and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of {{ill|Kadena Air Base|ja|嘉手納飛行場}}, so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}} and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar December 24|lt_ja=Lunar December24日}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar February 2|lt_ja=Lunar February2日}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar December 24|lt_ja=Lunar December24日}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar February 2|lt_ja=Lunar February2日}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}} and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.
== References ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== External Links == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town

revision.text:
'''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡}} {{ill|Chatan Town|ja|北谷町}} Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="注釈">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|Okinawa War|ja|沖縄戦}} and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of {{ill|Kadena Air Base|ja|嘉手納飛行場}}, so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}} and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.

In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.

The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.

The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.
== References ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== External Links == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town

'''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}} {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡}} {{ill|Chatan Town|ja|北谷町}} Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="注釈">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|Okinawa War|ja|沖縄戦}} and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of {{ill|Kadena Air Base|ja|嘉手納飛行場}}, so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}} and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.
== References ==

=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== External Links == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town

{{Okinawan Shrines}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Religious Facilities in Okinawa]] [[Category:Ryukyuan Religion]] [[Category:Buildings in Chatan Town]] [[Category:Ryukyu Shinto]] [[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[ja:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[sn:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[sn:Shimosedo Settlement Uganju]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shimosedo Settlement Uganju}}

'''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|12=draft}} {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|12=draft}} {{ill|Chatan Town|ja|北谷町|12=draft}} Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="注釈">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|12=draft}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|12=draft}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|Okinawa War|ja|沖縄戦|12=draft}} and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of {{ill|Kadena Air Base|ja|嘉手納飛行場|12=draft}}, so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|12=draft}} and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|12=draft}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|12=draft}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|12=draft}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=draft}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|12=draft}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|12=draft}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|12=draft}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875|12=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=draft}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.
== References ==

=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== External Links == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town

Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|12=draft}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|12=draft}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|12=draft}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=draft}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|12=draft}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|12=draft}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|12=draft}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875|12=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=draft}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.
== References ==

=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== External Links == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town

{{draft categories| [[Category:Religious Facilities in Okinawa]] [[Category:Ryukyuan Religion]] [[Category:Buildings in Chatan Town]] [[Category:Ryukyu Shinto]] [[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[sn:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[sn:Shimosedo Settlement Uganju]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shimosedo Settlement Uganju}}

'''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|12=draft}} {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|12=draft}} {{ill|Chatan Town|ja|北谷町|12=draft}} Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="注釈">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|12=draft}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|12=draft}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|Okinawa War|ja|沖縄戦|12=draft}} and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of {{ill|Kadena Air Base|ja|嘉手納飛行場|12=draft}}, so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|12=draft}} and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|12=draft}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|12=draft}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|12=draft}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=draft}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|12=draft}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|12=draft}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|12=draft}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875|12=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=draft}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

8 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:下勢頭集落の拝所 up to rev 89642790
revision.text:
'''下勢頭集落の拝所'''（しもせどしゅうらくのうがんじょ）は、[[沖縄県]][[中頭郡]][[北谷町]]上勢頭伊礼東原610番地<ref group="注釈">インターネット上での検索では、場所の特定ができない。本記事では北谷町の公式発表の通り記述する。</ref>にあり、かつての下勢頭集落で[[合祀]]されている[[拝所]]の一つである<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html 下勢頭集落の合祀所] - 北谷町</ref>。
== 概要 == かつての下勢頭集落の拝所が[[沖縄戦]]で焼失し、また戦後の[[嘉手納飛行場]]建設や開発事業などにより破壊されたため、現在の祭祀は合祀所で執り行われている<ref name=":1">北谷町史編集委員会『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』1992年 北谷町役場</ref>。
本記事では、[[第二次世界大戦]]前の下勢頭集落の拝所と、同所で行われていたウマチー（祭り）について述べる。
== アシビナージー（遊び庭岩） == かつての下勢頭集落と上勢頭集落の境界近くには、シードゥヌシー（シードゥ）と呼ばれる岩塔があり、その北側には下勢頭集落のアシビナー（遊び庭）があった<ref name=":0" />。戦前まで、[[12月24日 (旧暦)|旧暦12月24日]]のウガンブトゥチ（御顔解き）や[[2月2日 (旧暦)|旧暦2月2日]]のニングァチャー（クスユックイ・腰憩い）などの祈願の際には香炉が設けられ、アシビナージーの前で例祭が行われていたが、現在は上勢区公民館跡の祀に合祀されている<ref name=":1" />。
アシビナージーの名称は、シー（岩）の南側にあるアシビナーに由来しているという<ref name=":1" />。
=== ウガンブトゥチ（御顔解き） === 旧暦12月24日のウガンブトゥチには、ウチャヌク（餅）・ハナグミ（花米）・酒・白紙・線香を供え<ref name=":0" />、その年1年の神の加護に感謝（おふとち）し、来年の守護を祈願（願立て）した。また、疫病などが流行した時や兵役に服する者が入隊する際にはその無事を祈願することもあった。[[大東亜戦争]]中は出征兵士の母親や婦人会などが、月に1回[[六曜|大安]]の日に国体の安泰を祈願したとされている<ref name=":2">北谷町史編集委員会『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』1992年 北谷町役場</ref>。
=== ニングァチャー（腰憩い） === 旧暦2月2日、3日のニングァチャーは、豊年と集落民の健康と親和を祈願して、舞踊などの余興を奉納する習わしであった<ref name=":2" />。メーンダカリ（前村渠）とクシンダカリ（後村渠）というヤードゥ（宿）にわかれ、29歳までの若者青年組と30歳から49歳までの壮年組、50歳以上の老人組で集まり、大根の花やススキ等で飾った旗頭を振ってアシビナージーに向かった。アシビナーではアシビの神にご馳走を供え、余興を2日間にわたって催した。男性は病気や忌中以外の者は必ず参加しなくてはならず、理由なく欠席した場合は会費の徴収に応じなければならなかった<ref name=":4">下勢頭誌編集委員会『下勢頭誌 戦前編』 2001年 下勢頭郷友会</ref>。
== ハナグスク ヌ メーヌカー == かつて、このカー（井戸）は上勢頭から下勢頭へ通ずる[[中頭郡]]の南側、屋号下ノ花城家の屋敷前方にあったことからこのように呼ばれた<ref name=":4" />。現在は、｢南無諸大明神｣と陰刻された石碑のそばに設けてあるお通し所から、12月24日にウガンブトゥチの拝みが行われている<ref name=":0" />。
== ミジヌ神 == 下勢頭集落の屋号佐久川の東側には、村人が仕事から帰る際、農具を洗う池（ウフグムイ）があった。人々はこの池をミジヌ神（水の神）として崇めていた。現在は12月24日に郷友会の有志によってお通し拝みが行われている<ref name=":0" />。
== 徳川 == 徳川（トゥクガー）は、下勢頭集落から南方へ2キロメートルほどにある川である。[[1875年]]（[[明治]]8年）生まれの女性への聞き取りでは、若い頃に桶を頭の上に乗せて徳川まで水汲みに行ったとの証言がある。その後、徐々に家庭用井戸が増え始め、飲用に徳川を使用することはなくなったものの、牛馬の水浴びや洗濯等での使用は大東亜戦争直前まで行われていたという。徳川は、下勢頭の先人たちから長く集落の人々に使われ続けたため、命の水とされ、古くから拝所の一つとなっている<ref name=":4" />。
現在、徳川周辺から伊礼原B遺跡が発見され、遺跡公園が整備されるため、それに伴って川沿いに桜並木を植える計画がされている<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf 徳川周辺の公園整備について]}} - 北谷町</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
=== 出典 === {{Reflist}}
=== 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== 外部リンク == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html 下勢頭集落の合祀所] - 北谷町 * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf 徳川周辺の公園整備について]}} - 北谷町
{{デフォルトソート:しもせとしゆうらくのうかんしよ}} [[Category:沖縄県の宗教施設]] [[Category:琉球の信仰]] [[Category:北谷町の建築物]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[下勢頭集落の拝所]] to [[Shimosedo Settlement Uganju]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''下勢頭集落の拝所'''（しもせどしゅうらくのうがんじょ）は、[[沖縄県]][[中頭郡]][[北谷町]]上勢頭伊礼東原610番地<ref group="注釈">インターネット上での検索では、場所の特定ができない。本記事では北谷町の公式発表の通り記述する。</ref>にあり、かつての下勢頭集落で[[合祀]]されている[[拝所]]の一つである<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html 下勢頭集落の合祀所] - 北谷町</ref>。
== 概要 == かつての下勢頭集落の拝所が[[沖縄戦]]で焼失し、また戦後の[[嘉手納飛行場]]建設や開発事業などにより破壊されたため、現在の祭祀は合祀所で執り行われている<ref name=":1">北谷町史編集委員会『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』1992年 北谷町役場</ref>。
本記事では、[[第二次世界大戦]]前の下勢頭集落の拝所と、同所で行われていたウマチー（祭り）について述べる。
== アシビナージー（遊び庭岩） == かつての下勢頭集落と上勢頭集落の境界近くには、シードゥヌシー（シードゥ）と呼ばれる岩塔があり、その北側には下勢頭集落のアシビナー（遊び庭）があった<ref name=":0" />。戦前まで、[[12月24日 (旧暦)|旧暦12月24日]]のウガンブトゥチ（御顔解き）や[[2月2日 (旧暦)|旧暦2月2日]]のニングァチャー（クスユックイ・腰憩い）などの祈願の際には香炉が設けられ、アシビナージーの前で例祭が行われていたが、現在は上勢区公民館跡の祀に合祀されている<ref name=":1" />。
アシビナージーの名称は、シー（岩）の南側にあるアシビナーに由来しているという<ref name=":1" />。
=== ウガンブトゥチ（御顔解き） === 旧暦12月24日のウガンブトゥチには、ウチャヌク（餅）・ハナグミ（花米）・酒・白紙・線香を供え<ref name=":0" />、その年1年の神の加護に感謝（おふとち）し、来年の守護を祈願（願立て）した。また、疫病などが流行した時や兵役に服する者が入隊する際にはその無事を祈願することもあった。[[大東亜戦争]]中は出征兵士の母親や婦人会などが、月に1回[[六曜|大安]]の日に国体の安泰を祈願したとされている<ref name=":2">北谷町史編集委員会『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』1992年 北谷町役場</ref>。
=== ニングァチャー（腰憩い） === 旧暦2月2日、3日のニングァチャーは、豊年と集落民の健康と親和を祈願して、舞踊などの余興を奉納する習わしであった<ref name=":2" />。メーンダカリ（前村渠）とクシンダカリ（後村渠）というヤードゥ（宿）にわかれ、29歳までの若者青年組と30歳から49歳までの壮年組、50歳以上の老人組で集まり、大根の花やススキ等で飾った旗頭を振ってアシビナージーに向かった。アシビナーではアシビの神にご馳走を供え、余興を2日間にわたって催した。男性は病気や忌中以外の者は必ず参加しなくてはならず、理由なく欠席した場合は会費の徴収に応じなければならなかった<ref name=":4">下勢頭誌編集委員会『下勢頭誌 戦前編』 2001年 下勢頭郷友会</ref>。
== ハナグスク ヌ メーヌカー == かつて、このカー（井戸）は上勢頭から下勢頭へ通ずる[[中頭郡]]の南側、屋号下ノ花城家の屋敷前方にあったことからこのように呼ばれた<ref name=":4" />。現在は、｢南無諸大明神｣と陰刻された石碑のそばに設けてあるお通し所から、12月24日にウガンブトゥチの拝みが行われている<ref name=":0" />。
== ミジヌ神 == 下勢頭集落の屋号佐久川の東側には、村人が仕事から帰る際、農具を洗う池（ウフグムイ）があった。人々はこの池をミジヌ神（水の神）として崇めていた。現在は12月24日に郷友会の有志によってお通し拝みが行われている<ref name=":0" />。
== 徳川 == 徳川（トゥクガー）は、下勢頭集落から南方へ2キロメートルほどにある川である。[[1875年]]（[[明治]]8年）生まれの女性への聞き取りでは、若い頃に桶を頭の上に乗せて徳川まで水汲みに行ったとの証言がある。その後、徐々に家庭用井戸が増え始め、飲用に徳川を使用することはなくなったものの、牛馬の水浴びや洗濯等での使用は大東亜戦争直前まで行われていたという。徳川は、下勢頭の先人たちから長く集落の人々に使われ続けたため、命の水とされ、古くから拝所の一つとなっている<ref name=":4" />。
現在、徳川周辺から伊礼原B遺跡が発見され、遺跡公園が整備されるため、それに伴って川沿いに桜並木を植える計画がされている<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf 徳川周辺の公園整備について]}} - 北谷町</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
=== 出典 === {{Reflist}}
=== 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== 外部リンク == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html 下勢頭集落の合祀所] - 北谷町 * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf 徳川周辺の公園整備について]}} - 北谷町
{{デフォルトソート:しもせとしゆうらくのうかんしよ}} [[Category:沖縄県の宗教施設]] [[Category:琉球の信仰]] [[Category:北谷町の建築物]]

'''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Chatan Town|ja|北谷町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="Note">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|qq=draft|qq=draft}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|Okinawa War|ja|沖縄戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of {{ill|Kadena Air Base|ja|嘉手納飛行場|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.
== References ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}}
== External Links == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town

[[ja:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[sn:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[sn:Shimosedo Settlement Uganju]]

The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

[[ja:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[sn:下勢頭集落の拝所]] [[sn:Shimosedo Settlement Uganju]]

'''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Chatan Town|ja|北谷町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="Note">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|qq=draft|qq=draft}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|Okinawa War|ja|沖縄戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of {{ill|Kadena Air Base|ja|嘉手納飛行場|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Ryukyuan Religion]]
revision.text:
'''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakagami District|qq=}} {{ill|Chatan Town|ja|北谷町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chatan Town|qq=}} Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="Note">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship Halls|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|Okinawa War|ja|沖縄戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okinawa War|qq=}} and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of {{ill|Kadena Air Base|ja|嘉手納飛行場|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadena Air Base|qq=}}, so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}} and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of {{ill|Nakagami District|ja|中頭郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakagami District|qq=}}, which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1875|qq=}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.
== References ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}}
== External Links == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town
