'''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]] [[:en:Chatan, Okinawa|Chatan Town]] Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="Note">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship Halls|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Okinawa War]] and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of [[:en:Kadena Air Base|Kadena Air Base]], so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to [[:en:World War II|World War II]] and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]], which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1875|qq=}} ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.
== References ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}}
== External Links == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town
{{Okinawan Shrines}}

[[Category:Buildings in Chatan Town]] [[Category:Ryukyu Shinto]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }}

This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to [[:en:World War II|World War II]] and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]], which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1875|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1000}} ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship Halls|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Okinawa War]] and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of [[:en:Kadena Air Base|Kadena Air Base]], so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to [[:en:World War II|World War II]] and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]], which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in {{ill|1875|ja|1875|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1875|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1000}} ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Okinawa War]] and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of [[:en:Kadena Air Base|Kadena Air Base]], so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to [[:en:World War II|World War II]] and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]], which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in [[:en:1875|1875]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to [[:en:World War II|World War II]] and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]], which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in [[:en:1875|1875]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q48744705]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]] [[:en:Chatan, Okinawa|Chatan Town]] Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="Note">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship Halls|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Okinawa War]] and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of [[:en:Kadena Air Base|Kadena Air Base]], so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q48744705]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]] [[:en:Chatan, Okinawa|Chatan Town]] Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="Note">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship Halls|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Okinawa War]] and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of [[:en:Kadena Air Base|Kadena Air Base]], so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to [[:en:World War II|World War II]] and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]], which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in [[:en:1875|1875]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q48744705]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]] [[:en:Chatan, Okinawa|Chatan Town]] Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="Note">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship Halls|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Okinawa War]] and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of [[:en:Kadena Air Base|Kadena Air Base]], so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to [[:en:World War II|World War II]] and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]], which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in [[:en:1875|1875]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下勢頭集落の拝所]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q48744705]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]] [[:en:Chatan, Okinawa|Chatan Town]] Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="Note">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship Halls|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Okinawa War]] and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of [[:en:Kadena Air Base|Kadena Air Base]], so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to [[:en:World War II|World War II]] and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|qq=|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]], which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in [[:en:1875|1875]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|qq=|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

According to an interview with a woman born in [[:en:1875|1875]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下勢頭集落の拝所]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q48744705]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]] [[:en:Chatan, Okinawa|Chatan Town]] Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="Note">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship Halls|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Okinawa War]] and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of [[:en:Kadena Air Base|Kadena Air Base]], so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to [[:en:World War II|World War II]] and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]], which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in [[:en:1875|1875]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

According to an interview with a woman born in [[:en:1875|1875]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:下勢頭集落の拝所]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q48744705]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shimosedo Settlement Worship Hall''' (下勢頭集落の拝所) is located at [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]] [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]] [[:en:Chatan, Okinawa|Chatan Town]] Uesedoto Irei Higashihara 610-banchi<ref group="Note">Location cannot be determined through internet searches. In this article, the description follows the official announcement by Chatan Town.</ref> and is one of the {{ill|Worship Halls|ja|拝所|1=Haisho|lt=Uganju|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship Halls|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} that were subject to {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|lt=Joint Enshrinement|ja|合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=}} in the former Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0">[https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town</ref>.
== Overview == The former Shimosedo settlement worship hall was destroyed by fire during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Okinawa War]] and later demolished due to postwar construction and development projects such as the building of [[:en:Kadena Air Base|Kadena Air Base]], so that current ritual ceremonies are now held at the joint enshrinement hall<ref name=":1">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 下』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
This article describes the Shimosedo settlement worship hall prior to [[:en:World War II|World War II]] and the Umachī (festival) that was held there.
== Ashibinaajī (Asobiniwa Rock) == Near the boundary between the former Shimosedo settlement and the Uesedoto settlement, there stood a rock tower known as Shīdunushī (Shīdu), and to its north was the Ashibinā (playground) of the Shimosedo settlement<ref name=":0" />. Until before the war, during {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) and during {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} Ningāchā (Kusuyukui / Waist Rest) prayers, incense burners were set out and customary festivals were held before Ashibinaajī; however, they are now enshrined at the site of the former Uesedoto Community Center<ref name=":1" />.
The name “Ashibinaajī” is said to derive from the Ashibinā located on the southern side of the rock<ref name=":1" />.
=== Ugambutōchi (Divine Face Unveiling) === On {{ill|December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|12月24日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar December 24|lt_ja=Lunisolar December24日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 24 (Lunar Calendar)|1=24th day of the 12th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840461}} Ugambutōchi, offerings of Uchanuku (rice cakes), Hanagumi (flowered rice), sake, blank paper, and incense were presented<ref name=":0" />, as thanks for the year’s divine protection and in prayer for next year’s guardianship. In addition, when epidemics broke out or when individuals conscripted for military service enlisted, prayers for their safety were also offered. During the period of the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}, it is said that the mothers of conscripted soldiers and local women’s associations prayed for the stability of the national system on every month’s {{ill|Rokuyō|ja|六曜|lt=Taian|lt_ja=大安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokuyō|qq=}} day<ref name=":2">Chatan Town History Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『北谷町史 第三巻 資料編2 民俗 上』</nowiki>, 1992, Chatan Town Office</ref>.
=== Ningāchā (Waist Rest) === On {{ill|February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|ja|2月2日 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar February 2|lt_ja=Lunisolar February2日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 2 (Lunar Calendar)|1=2nd day of the 2nd month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839197}} and the following day, Ningāchā was a custom in which dances and other entertainments were offered in prayer for a bountiful harvest, the health of the settlement residents, and community harmony<ref name=":2" />. The participants were divided into the Meendakari (Former Village Canal) and Kushindakari (Latter Village Canal) divisions, with young people up to age 29, middle-aged groups from 30 to 49, and seniors aged 50 and above gathering and marching toward Ashibinaajī while waving banners decorated with radish flowers and pampas grass. At Ashibinā, feasts were offered to the Ashibi deity, and entertainments were held over the course of two days. All men, except those who were ill or in mourning, were required to participate; those absent without valid reason were subject to a fee<ref name=":4">Shimosedo Journal Editorial Committee, <nowiki>『下勢頭誌 戦前編』</nowiki>, 2001, Shimosedo Local Friends Association</ref>.
== Hanagusuku nu Mēnukā == In the past, this well (カー) was situated on the southern side of [[:en:Nakagami District, Okinawa|Nakagami District]], which connects Uesedoto and Shimosedo, in front of the mansion of the Shimono Hanashiro family, and was thus called by this name<ref name=":4" />. Currently, on December 24, prayers for Ugambutōchi are conducted from an entryway established beside a stone monument inscribed with “南無諸大明神”<ref name=":0" />.
== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in [[:en:1875|1875]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.

== Mijinu God == On the eastern side of the Sakugawa estate in the Shimosedo settlement, there was a pond (Ufugumui) used by villagers for washing their farming tools when returning from work. The people revered this pond as Mijinu God (Water God). Currently, on December 24, prayers are held at the entryway by volunteers from the local friends association<ref name=":0" />.
== Tokugawa == Tokugawa (トゥクガー) is a river located approximately 2 kilometers south of the Shimosedo settlement. According to an interview with a woman born in [[:en:1875|1875]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 8), she recalled that in her youth she would balance a bucket on her head while drawing water from the Tokugawa river. Later, as household wells gradually increased, the use of the Tokugawa for drinking water ceased, though it continued to be used for bathing cattle and horses as well as for laundry until just before the {{ill|Greater East Asia War|ja|大東亜戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater East Asia War|1=Greater East Asia War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3846875}}. Because Tokugawa had long been used by the forebears of Shimosedo, it came to be regarded as the water of life and has long been one of the worship halls<ref name=":4" />.
Currently, the Irei Hara B site has been discovered near the Tokugawa, and an archaeological park is being developed; accordingly, there is a plan to plant a row of cherry trees along the riverbank<ref name=":3">{{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town</ref>.
== References ==
=== Sources === {{Reflist}}
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}}
== External Links == * [https://www.chatan.jp/haisai/rekishi/bunkazai/uganju/shimoseido.html Joint Enshrinement Hall of the Shimosedo Settlement] - Chatan Town * {{PDFlink|[https://www.chatan.jp/choseijoho/machi_keikaku/kuwaeihei/master_plan.files/kukaku_masterplan01.pdf Park Development around Tokugawa]}} - Chatan Town
